title: The Old Chief's Sermon | Chapter 60 | The Story of John G. Paton
description:
<iframe title="YouTube video player" src="https://www.youtube-nocookie.com/embed/DWkHSVVdstc?si=VSfG1Y7QPgBx_vzG" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="allowfullscreen"></iframe>

The well was now finished. The place was neatly fenced in. And the old Chief said, "Missi, I think I could help you next Sabbath. Will you let me preach a sermon on the well?" "Yes," I at once replied, "if you will try to bring all the people to hear you." "Missi, I will try," he eagerly promised. The news spread like wildfire that the Chief Namakei was to be Missionary on the next day for the Worship, and the people, under great expectancy, urged each other to come and hear what he had to say. The old chief's sermon is a powerful example of the change that God has brought about.

title: Chapter 8: The Firstborn Dies—From Idolizing Her Child To Seeing God's Sovereign Power
description: In the spring of 1829, Mr. Boardman and his family embarked on a brief journey by sea to improve their health. Mrs. Boardman had recently suffered another bout of illness, and their young son, George, was delicate and weak. In fact, the only one in the family who seemed to be in good health was their first child, the darling Sarah, with her plump, rosy cheeks. She was her mother's greatest joy and pride, and yet, in the eyes of God, she was also her mother's idol. The loss of this beloved child was a devastating blow, but faith reassured her that it was delivered by the loving hand of a Father. Sometime later, she shared in a letter: "My ever dear Sister, it has been nearly eight months since our beloved Sarah passed away, and I haven't written to you since then. I've never gone this long without writing to you. For a while after her passing, it seemed little George was also close to leaving us, and I was bedridden for several weeks. Just as I began to recover, the uprising in Tavoy occurred, plunging us into chaos. This turmoil lasted until I fell ill again, about three months ago. I am only now beginning to recover. So, you can see, my dear sister, my circumstances have made it difficult to write. But that's not all. For some time after losing Sarah, I was too heartbroken to even think of writing. I was certain that God had taken her in love, and I knew she was now a joyful angel in heaven. However, the thought of never seeing her on earth again filled me with indescribable grief. Gradually, my mind found peace; not that I forgot her, but I realized, my dear Harriet, the deepest joys of this life are fleeting and leave us wanting. I no longer seek comfort in them as I once did. We have a strong, healthy boy now, but I'm careful not to idolize him as I did his dear sister. In her death, we witnessed the loss of what was most precious and beautiful, a lesson that seems to guard us against 'worshipping the creature'"  In another letter from around the same time, details about the child's illness and passing were shared:  "Our little Sarah left us on July 8th last year, at the age of 2 years and 8 months. She was an exceptionally beautiful child, with bright blue eyes, yellow hair, and rosy cheeks, which made her stand out vividly against the darker complexions around her. From the moment she became aware of her surroundings, she showered us with her deepest affection. The thought of displeasing us distressed her greatly, and she wouldn't find peace until she had apologized and received a forgiving kiss. She had learned to follow our directions without question. Whenever either of us was sick, she would gently caress our foreheads with her tiny hand and offer her tender kisses. Her love for her little brother George knew no bounds. From his birth until just two days before she passed, she adored him above all else. Three days before she died, as she lay uncomfortably in a large swing cradle and George was crying in the same room, we thought placing him next to her might comfort both, as he too was very ill. Delighted by the idea, she smiled, opened her little arms wide, and for the last time, embraced her beloved brother with all the love she had. This moment of joy seemed to momentarily ease her own suffering." "Little Sarah had an impressive command of languages for her young age. She spoke English exceptionally well and Burmese as fluently as any native speaker. Additionally, she could express herself in Hindostani and Karen to some extent. What's remarkable is that she never mixed the languages; she always spoke pure English with us and pure Burmese with the Burmese people. This ability to differentiate lasted as long as she could speak. She had memorized the Lord's Prayer and several short hymns. The verses by Dr. Judson on Mee Shawayee's passing were known to her by heart in Burmese, and she would often sing them for extended periods. " "These memories might seem minor to others, but I find myself reflecting on them for hours. It's only when I try to think more objectively that I convince myself these details might not hold interest for anyone else. Yet, I cherish the thought of my precious child, a 'sweet bud of immortality,' blossoming so gracefully before me. From these small glimpses, I imagine the full magnificence of her spirit, like anticipating the bloom of a perfect flower from its promising beginnings." "A few hours before she died, she called us to her, kissed us, and passed her dear hand, still full and dimpled as in health, softly over our faces. The pupils of her eyes were so dilated that she could not see us distinctly, and once, for a moment or two, her mind seemed to be wandering; then looking anxiously into my face, she said: 'I frightened, mamma! I frightened!' ... Oh with what feelings did I wash and dress her lovely form for the last time, and compose her perfect little limbs; and then see her—the dear child that had so long lain in my bosom—borne away to her newly-made grave. My heart grew faint when I thought that I had performed for her my last office of love; that she would never need a mother's hand again." "My dear husband conducted the funeral service with a heart full of sorrow, yet not without hope. The burial site is within our own property, about fifteen rods away from our home, nestled in a secluded spot surrounded by a grove of Gangau trees. Close by, there is a small sanctuary we built for private prayer. To this place we have frequently gone, seeking solace and communion. We believe that God, in His boundless wisdom who called our precious child to Himself, often joins us there, offering comfort and peace." The person who wrote the biography of Mrs. Boardman, who also succeeded her in the mission, pointed out that Mrs. Boardman once held a slight misconception in her faith regarding God's direct involvement in our lives. She struggled with the idea that the God who orchestrates the grand movements of the universe could also concern Himself with the trivial aspects of human existence, such as counting the hairs on our heads or monitoring the fall of a sparrow. This perspective made her question whether such minute attentiveness was beneath the dignity of the "Majesty of heaven and earth." However, the profound loss she experienced—the death of her child—radically changed her outlook. Through this painful event, she vividly saw the hand of a loving Father disciplining His child. She deeply felt that she had been corrected for valuing God's gift (her child) more than God Himself, the Giver. This realization led her to a powerful and unshakeable belief that even the smallest details of her life were under God's watchful care and direction, just as much as the vast expanse of the starry sky. She understood that her personal growth in faith and the specific means by which it was achieved were meticulously planned by God's sovereign grace and wisdom. From that moment on, Mrs. Boardman never again doubted God's intimate involvement in every aspect of her life. It's appropriate now to elaborate on the profoundly moving events that took place a few months following the child's passing, events which were hinted at in the first of the two letters mentioned earlier. Listen to the full audiobook here.

title: "I don’t know if God will accept me"?
excerpt: So, where is there room for the objection, “I don’t know if God will accept me”? Such a person knows that if they go to Christ, they will be accepted, and if they stay away, they will not be accepted. Yet, somehow, this does not satisfy them—in fact, it discourages them.
description:
Some people are also held back from becoming Christians because they doubt that God would accept them. When we urge those who seek salvation to turn to God, cutting off all other hopes and telling them to repent and believe the Gospel without delay, they often feel troubled, saying they are such great sinners that they doubt God would ever accept them. Such people seek more reassurance than even the Cross of Christ offers. The Cross reveals the fullness and freeness of salvation. By God’s own authority, it invites and urges them to come to Christ to receive life. It shows that the basis of their acceptance lies not in themselves, but entirely in Christ’s work. It promises that the greatest sinner, as well as the least, if he comes to Jesus, will find a warm and ready acceptance with God, because the size of one’s sins does not affect acceptance. God simply requires them to accept His way of mercy and receive Jesus as offered in the Gospel. So, where is there room for the objection, “I don’t know if God will accept me”? Such a person knows that if they go to Christ, they will be accepted, and if they stay away, they will not be accepted. Yet, somehow, this does not satisfy them—in fact, it discourages them. What they really seek is comfort in their sin, wanting assurance that there is some promise in God’s word for those who remain anxious yet unrepentant. This is why they seek more encouragement than the Cross can give. The Cross cannot encourage anyone who stays away from Christ, grieves His Spirit, and continues in a willful rebellion, even if it is an anxious and remorseful one. Such people claim to be seeking the kingdom of heaven, yet often feel unfriendly toward Jesus Christ, even admitting they prefer death over life. Consciously or not, this is their real condition. And still, they are anxious for salvation. But what does the anxiety of those who reject the Gospel really mean, except a desperate desire to avoid hell while still clinging to their distance from God? This is the root of their struggle, and we cannot resolve it—nor should we attempt to if we could. This is their excuse for not becoming Christians. And who can answer it? As long as people continue in this way, their case remains hopeless. The longer they stay in this state, the farther they drift from becoming Christians, and the less likely they are to turn to Christ at all. There are still others who say, “I cannot become a Christian.” But you don’t mean it is impossible, even with God’s help, for you to become a changed person. If that were the case, then all these lessons from the Cross would be in vain; in vain would God have sent His Son to die, His Spirit to convict, His ordinances to awaken, and His Word to urge you to repentance, for then, you must “die in your sins.” More likely, you mean that in your current state of mind, with your present attitude, you find it impossible to repent and believe the Gospel. This is clear enough: as long as you are God’s enemy, you cannot be His friend; as long as you love sin, you cannot turn from it; while you reject Christ, you cannot come to Him. It is truly impossible to love and hate, to accept and reject, at the same time. But is this state of hostility and unbelief a right state of mind, and can it be justified? If not, and if this is your only barrier to becoming a Christian, then why do you hold onto it? Why, despite all instruction, correction, and appeals—all the calls of love and mercy, the promptings of God’s Spirit, and the convictions of your own conscience—do you gather every argument to defend it? Why not yield to these calls, admitting that this sinful mindset is no excuse? God does, and rightly so, call you to embrace a different attitude, one that aligns with what you already know to be your duty. It’s hard to see how anyone could be “condemned out of his own mouth” more clearly than through such reasoning as this. Perhaps you will respond that you are aware of this, and while you know your sinful state of mind is wrong, you feel you cannot overcome it. That is a different matter entirely. If you recognize this truth—that your last and strongest excuse condemns you—then isn’t it astonishing that you still cling to it? Isn’t it astonishing that you try to deceive yourself by defending a position in which you yourself have no confidence? Wouldn’t it be better to stand silent, as you will surely be on the last day, if this is all you have to plead? Wouldn’t it be better to admit you have no excuse, to humble yourself before God in deep self-reproach, and cry out, “Guilty! Guilty! Lost! Lost! Lord, save me, or I perish!” Overcoming this state of mind is difficult—an obstacle that only God’s mighty grace can remove. It’s a tragic truth that the tendency to sin is extremely strong in the human heart, and that no one has ever come to true godliness without painfully sensing the opposition of their own heart to God, and their complete dependence on the Holy Spirit. All genuine conviction rests on this awareness. But this is not your position. Instead, you are using your dependence on the Spirit as an excuse, as a reason to justify your refusal to become a Christian. A person who is truly sincere in their need never does this. The very fact that you’re using it as a main excuse for remaining in unbelief reveals that you’re not sincerely feeling its humbling reality. Would to God that you truly felt this—so deeply that it would strip you of self-confidence, turn your strength to weakness, and your hopes to utter reliance on almighty grace. Only then would you understand the truth of “Without Christ, I can do nothing.” Heaven is far beyond your reach, but there is a way, like the ladder Jacob saw, by which you can ascend—even as lowly as you are—to God’s dwelling place. Christ Himself has opened the way into the holiest place through His blood. “I am the way,” He says; “no one comes to the Father except through me.” If you say you cannot come to Christ without divine help, this is true as well. The Savior Himself says, “No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him.” You cannot feel this truth too deeply. God “will have mercy on whom He will have mercy.” He is not obligated to make you “willing in the day of His power.” You are in His hands, like clay in the hands of the potter. He may leave you to your own chosen path of destruction. He has every right to do so and may be provoked to do so by your repeated excuses. Only when you recognize this sovereign right of God will you have a true view of yourself as a lost sinner and a defiant rebel against the King of the universe. If you could grasp this—if you truly felt your own vileness, guilt, and helplessness, so that you were humbled to the dust before God, feeling that you are sinking in deep waters and that only almighty grace can lift you from the “horrible pit” and place your feet on solid ground—then these flimsy excuses would appear to you as “refuges of lies.” Then there would be hope for you; you would not be far from the kingdom of heaven. If you could once “glory in your weakness,” so that the power of Christ might rest upon you, you would no longer complain about the difficulty of becoming a Christian. Instead, you would wonder why you had not come to Him sooner.
The work is done when you truly realize that, though you are utterly weak, you have God’s omnipotence to rely on. Burdened by sin, oppressed with doubt and fear, blinded by spiritual darkness, and deeply troubled—if, under these burdens and struggles, you can come to the Cross, you will not be turned away empty. Tears, sighs, and a broken heart are welcome at the throne of grace. “When the poor and needy seek water and there is none, and their tongue fails for thirst, I the Lord will hear them; I the God of Israel will not forsake them.”
These are some of the sinner’s excuses for continuing in unbelief. Do they hold up in view of the Cross? Do they satisfy his own conscience? Will they stand at the bed of death? Will he be able to plead them at the judgment? Can he truly find any valid reason for not becoming a Christian? Isn’t it clear that the most reasonable thing he could do is to stop resisting God and to yield to the claims of redeeming love? Isn’t there some strange, blinding delusion clouding his mind? When he reasons this way, isn’t it because his understanding is darkened, his judgment blurred, his reason misled? No reasonable person makes such mistakes when it comes to their earthly interests, so why are they so irrational when it comes to eternal matters? Could it be that the great adversary has more influence over this mindset than people realize? Is he not actively working to prevent the Gospel’s impact, to blind the minds of those who refuse to believe? It’s hard to understand how people who are capable of sound reasoning stray so far from the truth and arrive at such strange conclusions about personal faith. The Cross of Christ solemnly warns against these self-deceptions. In the next life, being led astray by these delusions will offer no comfort; instead, you will wonder how your usual caution and wisdom could have failed you so completely. It is a fearful thing to harden your heart, adding sin to sin, until you become a vessel of wrath set for destruction. Soon, you must pass from these days of mercy to the day of judgment, from the light of time to the brighter light of eternity. Abandon, then, these indefensible fortresses, these weak defenses of the carnal mind, these “refuges of lies,” and flee to the hope set before you in the Gospel. Submit to the authority of the Cross, be drawn by its love, and receive the Savior. Instead of resisting Omnipotence and grieving His Spirit, lift your eyes to Him in hope and desire. Then the dark clouds will lift, the Sun of Righteousness will shine, and you will find peace with God through Jesus Christ. No longer will you embody what should never exist in a world of rational beings—a wicked person rebelling against a good God, a frail creature contending with an all-powerful Creator, a miserable soul rejecting its only path to joy, a lost sinner turning away from the only Savior, a rational mind questioning the wisdom of a salvation that honors the infinite love and wisdom of God.

title: The Christmas Pudding That Made an Atheist Believe
description:
The following story shows how deeply the children absorbed the spirit of faith that filled the home. For them, praying and watching for God’s answer was simply part of life. But not everyone in the house was so easily convinced. Lydia, the servant maid, thought this “faith business” was a poor foundation for managing a household—even though she had to admit there was always food on the table and her wages were never late.

Lydia was fiercely loyal to Miss Soltau, though in a dramatic and emotional way. She longed to give her mistress the kind of luxuries and comforts that clearly weren’t part of God’s plan. One evening, while carrying supper to a sick child, Lydia made her feelings known. The little one had begun chatting cheerfully about Christmas and the wonderful puddings they would soon enjoy.

“There ain’t going to be no pudding,” Lydia said bluntly. “Miss Soltau’s too poor to buy currants and raisins.”

Though startled, the child quickly rallied and caught Lydia off guard.

“Lydia,” she said, “let’s have a secret. When you bring my tray at night, we’ll pray for currants—and see if they come. Don’t let’s tell anyone.”

Lydia eagerly agreed. The idea suited her perfectly—she would be part of a private plan to supply the family’s needs. And if currants did come (and they often did, somehow), she could feel proud of her role in the miracle.

So the two prayed faithfully—but no currants came. As Christmas neared, there was still no sign of an answer.

Then, one night, Miss Soltau returned home near midnight after tending her sick mother. At the door, an excited Lydia met her, breathless.

“Please, ma’am, there’s a parcel come, and I think it’s the currants! The man just put it down and went away.”

Miss Soltau, used to Lydia’s flair for drama, calmed her.

“I’m expecting parcels for China,” she said. “Leave it in the hall and go to bed. You should have been there long ago.”

She went into her study to write some letters. But soon, Lydia appeared again—this time in tears.

“Oh, Miss, please open the parcel. I can’t sleep till I know if it’s the currants.”

Miss Soltau, unaware of the secret prayer pact, was ready to deal firmly with Lydia’s outburst. But amid her sobs, Lydia blurted out the whole story. Together, they opened the parcel—and found twenty pounds of currants. Now both were thrilled. They agreed to say nothing until the children were gathered for evening prayers. Then the bale would be revealed.

The little sick girl, bundled in her dressing gown, was carried downstairs. A cheer of joy filled the room when the children saw the pile of dried fruit. Lydia beamed with pride, knowing she had played a part. Her young prayer partner, with a mixture of awe and certainty, clapped her hands and said:

“You see, Lydia, I knew God would send it.”

After the laughter and thanksgiving, Miss Soltau reminded them gently, “A pudding can’t be made from currants alone.” So together, they prayed—listing before their Heavenly Father the ingredients still missing: flour, sugar, spice, suet, raisins, and more.

The days until Christmas were few. Each afternoon, the children came running in from school asking:

“Has the pudding come yet?”

Just before Christmas, it did. During evening prayers, as Miss Soltau was reading a Psalm, a knock came at the door. Charlie jumped up and said:

“Maybe it’s the pudding!”

Lydia went to open the door and returned dragging a large hamper. Charlie cried out:

“Put away the Bibles—it’s the pudding!”

The room erupted in joyful noise as they opened the basket. Inside was everything needed: flour, sugar, suet, raisins, spices—and even oranges and cakes for everyone. Lydia, now fully convinced, declared herself a firm believer in the power of prayer. From that day forward, she never again doubted the foundation of faith that upheld the household.

There was no clue to the sender. The delivery man simply said:

“There is no name, and no answer.”

There were many times when no clue ever came about who had sent a gift. But in this case, the mystery was solved five months later.

One evening, Miss Soltau was struggling through the dark, fighting a strong southwest wind to reach the pillar-box. There, she saw a woman—frail and elderly—also trying to post a letter. Though Miss Soltau recognized her as someone from Wellington Square, she didn’t know her name. Seeing her struggle against the wind, Miss Soltau offered to walk her home. The woman gratefully accepted.

As they walked, the lady asked, “Aren’t you the one who lives with all the little children?”

“I am,” Miss Soltau replied. “Their parents are missionaries.”

The woman brightened with interest. “Then I should take this chance to apologize for sending a bale of currants without any note. My husband has relatives in Spain who sent him a large shipment of dried fruits last Christmas—far more than we could ever use. We often watch your children running off to school, and I said to him, ‘That’s the kind of household that could use a bale of currants.’ So, we sent them over.”

“You can hardly imagine the joy they brought to my little family,” Miss Soltau said.

The stormy wind interrupted the rest of the conversation, but the woman managed to explain further. After sending the currants, she had felt moved to supply everything else the children would need for a proper Christmas treat—so the second parcel had followed.

“You must forgive me,” she said, “for the way it was done. Not knowing you personally, I wasn’t sure how to send a gift to the children—so I sent it anonymously. But now that we’ve met, I do hope to call on you soon.”

Before they parted, Miss Soltau asked if the children might be allowed to come and thank their kind benefactors in person. The next day, dressed in their best clothes and each having memorized a Scripture verse, the children walked across the square to pay their visit. They were ushered into the room where Mr. Kay, an invalid, lay resting on a sofa.

He listened with deep interest as Miss Soltau shared her side of the story. He was visibly moved by the sight of so many children who, at such little cost to himself, had received such great joy. As he asked about each child’s parents, he heard stories of remarkable devotion and sacrifice for the sake of the Gospel.

Once the ice was broken, the children couldn’t stop talking. They told him about little Emma and her struggle with rheumatic fever, about Lydia’s blunt disbelief, and about the secret prayer for currants—and the surprising answer that had come.

Mr. Kay was shaped by the rationalist thinking of the 19th century, where science was often seen as standing in opposition to belief in divine providence. In those days, the lines were clear—many who rejected Christianity openly called themselves atheists. Mr. Kay had placed himself firmly among the free-thinkers.

Yet now, lying there, he looked at Miss Soltau—her face glowing with a heavenly light—and at the children who so naturally viewed him as a messenger from God. In the background, he pictured their parents, living with courage and faith on the far side of the world.

And in that moment, the walls of unbelief he had built began to collapse. The man who had once said in his heart, “There is no God,” found himself overcome by a Presence he could not deny. For the first time in his life, he bowed inwardly before the God he had long rejected.

title: Struggling to Find a Text to Preach!
description:
From: Charles Spurgeon Autobiography, Chapter 19 - The Boy-preacher of Fens

In my earlier years, I came across a statement in a book of lectures on preaching that deeply troubled me. It said something like this: “If a man struggles to choose a text, he should go back to the shop or the farm—he clearly lacks the ability to be a minister.” Since I often found choosing a text to be a heavy burden, I began to wonder if I should give up preaching and take up some other kind of work. But I have not done so, because I still firmly believe that God has clearly confirmed my calling, even if that lecturer’s harsh words seemed to disqualify me.

That remark weighed so heavily on my conscience that I went to my grandfather—who had been preaching for fifty years—and asked if he ever had trouble picking a subject. He answered honestly that it had always been his greatest difficulty, and that preaching itself was no trouble in comparison. I remember his words: “The problem isn’t that there aren’t enough texts, but that there are so many. I feel torn between them.” We are like a lover of fine flowers, surrounded by the best in the garden, told to pick just one. He lingers between the rose and the lily, unable to choose just one from among thousands of beauties.

I still find this to be true. Choosing a text is a real struggle for me—not because there’s a lack, but because there’s an abundance. The French call it embarras de richesse—an embarrassment of riches. It’s not confusion from having too little, but from having too much—so many truths calling for attention, so many duties to preach, and so many spiritual needs to address.

Often I spend long hours in prayer and waiting just to find the right subject—that is the biggest part of my preparation. I’ve worked hard over many ideas, pondered points of doctrine, shaped outlines from verses, and then buried those outlines as if they never existed. I drift for hours, as if lost at sea, until at last I see the guiding light and can set sail directly toward the goal. I believe that almost every Saturday I come up with enough outlines to last me a month—if I felt free to preach them. But I dare not use them, any more than an honest sailor would bring illegal goods to shore.

I am always sure to have the most happy day when I get a good text in the morning from my Master. When I have had to preach two or three sermons in a day, I have asked Him for the morning subject, and preached from it; and I have asked Him for the afternoon’s topic or the evening’s portion, and preached from it, after meditating on it for my own soul’s comfort — not in the professional style of a regular sermon-maker, but feasting upon it for myself. Such simple food has done the people far more good than if I had been a week in manufacturing a sermon, for it has come warm from the heart just after it has been received in my own soul; and therefore it has been well spoken, because well known, well tasted, and well felt. Sometimes, my texts have come to me in a very remarkable way. While I was living at Cambridge, I had, as usual, to preach in the evening at a neighboring village, to which I had to walk. After reading and meditating all day, I could not meet with the right text. Do what I would, no response came from the sacred oracle, no light flashed from the Urim and Thummim; I prayed, I searched the Scriptures, I turned from one verse to another, but my mind would not take hold of a text; I was, as Bunyan would say, “much tumbled up and down in my thoughts.” Just then, I walked to the window, and looked out. On the other side of the narrow street in which I lived, I saw a poor solitary canary bird upon the slates, surrounded by a crowd of sparrows, who were all pecking at it as if they would tear it to pieces. At that moment the verse came to my mind, “Mine heritage is unto me as a speckled bird, the birds round about are against her.” I walked off with the greatest possible composure, considered the passage during my long and lonely walk, and preached upon the peculiar people, and the persecutions of their enemies, with freedom and ease to myself, and I believe with comfort to my rustic audience. The text was sent to me, and if the ravens did not bring it, certainly the sparrows did.

title: From London Parlors to Newgate’s Cells: Elizabeth Fry’s Bold Mission
description: The following is Chapter 10 of Twelve Wonderful Women by E. H. Farrance. Please note that we do not yet have an audiobook of this title on our site. During a visit to London as a young girl, Elizabeth Gurney met an elderly friend, Deborah Darby. Upon seeing the sincere and thoughtful girl, Deborah said she would one day be “a light to the blind, speech to the mute, and feet to the lame.” These words deeply moved Elizabeth and were later fulfilled in remarkable ways. Elizabeth Gurney was born in Norwich on May 21, 1780, one of ten children. Her parents were Quakers. Her father was a banker, known for his broad mind and sharp intellect. Her mother was a well-educated and refined woman, whose death left a deep sorrow in Elizabeth’s early life. At a yearly Quaker meeting, the preaching of William Savery stirred Elizabeth’s heart. She began to grow tired of her life of comfort and fun, and started to long for something deeper. She gave up music, dancing, and outward finery, and chose to wear the plain clothing of strict Quaker tradition. In 1800, Elizabeth married Joseph Fry, a junior partner in a bank. They lived at St. Mildred’s Court in London, which also served as the place of business. Her life quickly became full and busy. The Frys kept an open home for guests. As time went on, children were born. With a large household to manage, guests to care for, frequent letters exchanged with her sisters, and set times of quiet reflection, her days were full. She was appointed by the Quakers to visit the school and workhouse in Islington. Yet, she still felt restless. She longed for a greater field of service, for more direct work among the poor. After Joseph’s father passed away, they moved to the family’s old and lovely home in Plashet, Essex. After the noise and bustle of London, the peace and beauty of the countryside brought her great joy. God was gently guiding His servant, as He always does when His faithful children long to follow His will. In 1813, at the request of a friend, Elizabeth Fry made her first visit to Newgate Prison, accompanied by the sister of Sir Fowell Buxton. At that time, the condition of prisons was terrible. Everyone was kept together—young and old, criminals, people with mental illness, debtors, and even children. There was hardly any bedding, and no coal or candles were provided. The women were wild and desperate, the air was foul, and the level of misery and sin was beyond description. Alcohol was sold freely to anyone with money. The entire system was full of bribery and corruption, and many of the jailers were no better in character than the prisoners. When Elizabeth and her friend arrived, they found about three hundred women, many with children, crowded into filthy rooms. There was no order or work provided, and they were all under the care of just a man and his son. The women cooked, washed, and slept on the floor. In spite of how dreadful the place was, Elizabeth Fry and her friend went into the cells and spoke kindly to the women. Her compassionate heart was deeply moved, and she longed to do something lasting to help them. The second time Elizabeth Fry visited Newgate, she asked to be left alone with the women, and her request was granted. She read to them, spoke about the love of the Saviour, and then asked for their help in starting a school for the children. She was deeply moved by their response and their clear desire to improve life for their children. For a time, illness and sorrow in her own family kept her from returning, and all she could do was send regular gifts of clothing to the poor prisoners. In 1817, Mrs. Fry resumed her work. The women in the prison welcomed her with tears of joy when she offered to teach their children. They even chose one of their own to lead the school. Elizabeth and a faithful group of helpers gave themselves fully to the work, though the City Magistrates strongly discouraged them. Still, the effort was made, and the results were remarkable: no longer was the prison filled with wild, half-dressed, drunken women demanding charity. Instead, it became quiet—no longer echoing with foul language, curses, or immoral songs. Between 1818 and her final illness in 1841, Elizabeth Fry—except for one occasion—visited every ship that left England carrying women convicts to Botany Bay, seeking to ease their harsh conditions. Her work was praised in both the House of Commons and the House of Lords. It was demanding work, and her daughters often helped her manage the many letters she received from across the country concerning her efforts at Newgate. She also endured much personal hardship and pain in her struggle to bring an end to capital punishment for the crime of forgery. Here is an example of Elizabeth Fry’s remarkable influence. Years later, a woman in Sydney confessed that during one of Mrs. Fry’s talks—what she called a sermon—she had tried not to listen by silently counting to herself. As Mrs. Fry was saying goodbye to the prisoners, she called this young woman aside. Placing her hand on the woman’s shoulder, she said slowly and firmly, “Let not thine eyes covet.” She said nothing more. “Her voice was quiet and solemn; kind like a mother, yet firm like a judge.” When the young woman arrived in the colony, she behaved well for a time. But one day, while looking into her mistress’s box, a gold thimble caught her eye. She slipped it onto her finger at once. Just as she was about to close the lid, she suddenly felt Mrs. Fry’s hand on her shoulder again—the same gentle, firm touch. She looked around in fear, but found herself alone. Overwhelmed, she put the thimble back and trembled with fright. The secret of Mrs. Fry’s influence lay in her deep sense of God’s presence. She often wrote in her journal, “Thou hast been my stronghold, my Rock and my Fortress.” Though she worked tirelessly to improve the prisoners’ outward conditions, she never failed to share with them the message of salvation through the death of the Lord Jesus Christ. A friend who once accompanied Mrs. Fry to Newgate and witnessed her reading the Scriptures to the women wrote this account: “After a few minutes of silent prayer, she quietly opened the Bible and turned to Isaiah chapter 53. Her manner was deeply reverent. She read with such clear, gentle, and well-measured speech that not a word was lost. As she read, her mind seemed fully focused on the passage and nothing else. She spoke with gentle strength about the amazing love of God.” The response from the women was powerful. They deeply loved and respected her. Yet, despite the honor she received, Mrs. Fry remained truly humble, always praying for help and strength. In 1825 and 1826, the Fry family faced serious financial trouble and had to leave their home at Plashet. But the woman who had trusted God in good times now showed she could also trust Him in hardship and accept His will. Her prison reform efforts did not stop with Newgate. They spread to many towns across England and even into Ireland. She felt deeply when some of her children chose to join churches outside the Society of Friends. Yet, with gracious Christian love, she said: “If, after seeking to know God’s will—through His Spirit in your hearts and the teachings of Scripture—you believe you should join another church, then I bid you God-speed in this.” Elizabeth Fry was invited to meet Her Majesty, the young Queen, soon to be married to Prince Albert. She found the Queen to be interested and kind-hearted. Elizabeth continued to travel widely in connection with her work. Later, a season of long and painful illness came. But throughout it all, her faith and trust in God remained firm. Then the call came for this faithful servant, and she “passed away to be with Christ.” Elizabeth Fry was a woman who walked closely with the Lord each day. Because of that, she could hear His voice clearly and fully surrendered herself to His leading.

title: “I Killed My Own Children”: How the Gospel Ended Infanticide on One Island
description: Excerpt taken from: Woman’s Work in the Mission Field—or, The Special Ministry of Christian Women to the Heathen But the victories of the Gospel more than made up for the times of sorrow and discouragement. One by one, the cruel customs of heathen life were abandoned—among them, the terrible practice of infanticide. This was once common throughout Raiatea. Mrs. Williams employed a servant whose former role in society had been to kill infants. After her conversion to Christ, she became a new person—devoted, honest, faithful, and kind. She served Mrs. Williams and her children loyally for fifteen years. At times, with tears in her eyes, she would tell Mrs. Williams how she had once carried out these awful acts. Sometimes she would smother a newborn with a wet cloth. Other times she would pinch the infant’s throat until it died. In some cases, she would bury the baby alive under earth and stones deep in the forest. One of the most brutal methods she described was breaking the joints of the child—first the fingers and toes, then, if the baby lived, the ankles and wrists, then elbows and knees—and if it still lived, she would end its suffering by strangling it. She spoke of these past deeds with deep sorrow and repentance, as did many mothers who had taken the lives of their children before they came to know the truth. One woman admitted to killing sixteen children—all her own. Another confessed to nine, another to seven, and another to five. But after the Gospel came to Raiatea, this terrible sin was mourned with great grief. At one school celebration, where six hundred children stood before the king, something deeply moving happened. An elderly chief stood up, overcome with emotion, and cried out: “I must speak. Oh, if only I had known the Gospel was coming! If I had known these blessings were on their way, I would have saved my children. They would have been among this joyful group, repeating these precious words. But I destroyed them all. I have no children left. I was the father of nineteen, and every one of them is gone.” Then, turning to the king—his relative—he said with deep sorrow, “You, my brother, saw me kill child after child. But you never stopped me, never said, ‘Wait, brother! God is about to bless us; the Gospel is coming to our land.’” During Mr. and Mrs. Williams’ time in Raiatea, a young woman who served as a teacher in one of the missionary schools had an extraordinary story of being rescued from death years earlier. Her mother and father were of different social ranks in Raiatean society, and tradition required that all their children be put to death at birth. The first child was born and killed, and the second met the same fate—even though the father pleaded for its life. He clearly felt love for his children, and when their third child, a healthy baby girl, was born, he once again begged that she be allowed to live. But the mother and her relatives refused. While the father was away in the mountains, they buried the newborn alive. When he returned and learned what had happened, he rushed to the burial site, dug up the child, and found her still alive. He rescued her and gave her into the care of his brother and sister, who took the baby to Eimeo, an island about seventy miles away, where infanticide was not practiced. The father soon died and had never told the mother that their child had been saved. Some time later, Christianity reached Raiatea, and the mother became a believer. As her heart was awakened by the Gospel, she deeply mourned the children she had lost. Then one day, a woman revealed to her that one of her children had been spared. The mother immediately traveled to Eimeo—and there, to her overwhelming joy, she found her daughter alive and well. The girl had grown into a fine young woman, and she became her mother’s support and joy. She also became a sincere Christian and faithfully served as a teacher in the women’s school.

title: “I’m Half Alligator, Half Snapping Turtle!” — The Wildest Wife God Ever Changed
description:
Within the bounds of my district lived a local preacher, a small, kind-hearted, and pleasant man. He was known for his piety and was considered a good and useful preacher. However, his wife was his complete opposite in nearly every way—except that she was believed to be virtuous. She was high-tempered, domineering, quarrelsome, and fiercely opposed to religion.

She refused to prepare his clothes when he went out to preach and would not allow him to say a blessing at the table or pray with the family. When he tried to pray, she would cause as much disturbance as possible—turning over chairs while he read, sang, or prayed. If nothing else worked, she would even grab a cat and throw it in his face while he was kneeling in prayer.

The poor man was tormented nearly to the point of despair. He had invited several preachers to his home, hoping they could talk to her and calm her down, but it was all in vain. She cursed them to their faces and raged like a madwoman.

He had often urged me to visit, but to be honest, I was reluctant. I felt deep pity for him, as did everyone who knew his situation. But finally, after much insistence, I agreed to go home with him one evening, planning to stay the night.

As soon as we arrived, I could tell she was furious. It was as if the devil himself was in her, as big as an alligator. I made up my mind right then about how I would handle the situation.

After supper, her husband gently said, “Come, wife, stop what you’re doing and let us have prayer.”

At that, she exploded in anger. “I will have none of your praying around me!” she shouted.

I tried to speak to her calmly, reasoning with her, but the more I spoke, the angrier she became. She cursed me bitterly.

At that moment, I put on a stern look and said firmly, “Madam, if you were my wife, I would break you of your bad ways—or I would break your neck.”

“The devil I would!” she shouted. “You’re a fine Christian, aren’t you?” Then she let loose a volley of curses that were almost unbearable.

“Be still,” I said firmly. “We must and will have prayer.”

But she swore we would not.

“If you don’t calm down and behave yourself,” I warned, “I’ll put you out of doors.”

At this, she clenched her fists and swore, “I’m half alligator and half snapping turtle, and it’ll take a better man than you to put me out!”

We were in a small cabin, not far from the open door. Without hesitation, I grabbed her by the arm, swung her around in a circle, and sent her right out the door.

She sprang up, tore at her hair, and cursed with a fury I had rarely seen matched. The door was built strong to keep out hostile Indians, so I shut it tight, barred it, and went to prayer. Though my emotions were hard to describe, I was determined to win this battle or die trying.

Outside, she raged and screamed, but inside, I lifted my voice in a spiritual song, trying to drown her out. The five or six little children, terrified, ran and hid under the beds. Poor things—they were scared nearly to death.

I kept singing, and she kept raging, until finally, she exhausted herself and could hardly catch her breath. At last, she fell silent, then knocked at the door.

“Mr. Cartwright, please let me in,” she said.

“Will you behave yourself if I let you in?” I asked.

“Oh yes,” she answered.

Keeping my guard up, I opened the door, took her by the hand, and led her inside. She sat near the fireplace, drenched in sweat and pale as death.

After a moment, she sighed and said, “Oh, what a fool I am!”

“Yes,” I said, “one of the biggest fools I have ever seen in my life. And now, you must repent of this, or you will surely go to the devil in the end.”

She sat in silence.

Turning to the children, I said, “Come out now. Your mother won’t hurt you.” Then I looked at her husband and said, “Brother C., let us pray again.”

We knelt down, and both of us prayed. This time, she sat quietly, as gentle as a lamb.

And now, dear reader, though this was one of the hardest cases I had ever encountered, I must give glory to God. In less than six months after this battle with the devil, this woman was soundly converted. If ever there was a changed soul, it was hers. As her children grew, I believe all of them found salvation, and the entire family became a happy and godly household. She, who had once been so bold for the cause of the wicked one, became just as fearless in the cause of Christ.

Taken from Chapter 20 of Peter Cartwright's biography.

title: From Ashamed to Unashamed: Cranmer’s Last Confession
description:
In 1556, Mary and her Roman Catholic allies carried out a plan they had long been preparing. Mary had never forgiven Archbishop Cranmer for his role in her mother’s divorce or for his leadership in the Reformation. Though he had reluctantly supported the cause of Lady Jane Grey—making himself guilty of treason—this alone would likely have been pardoned. But the queen’s hatred and religious zeal were too deep to allow for mercy.

She had long intended to make Cranmer her victim. To satisfy both her own vengeance and the demands of Rome, she chose to prosecute him for heresy rather than treason. Cranmer was summoned by the Pope to appear in Rome and answer charges of heresy. But as he was being held in strict confinement at Oxford, he had no way to respond, even if he had wished to. As a result, the Papal Court condemned him as rebellious for failing to appear.

The queen then sent Bishop Bonner of London and Bishop Thirleby of Ely to Oxford to formally strip Cranmer of his office. The degradation ceremony was carried out with careful detail. Bonner, eager to humiliate Cranmer, went beyond what Thirleby could approve. At the end of it, Cranmer was dressed in a worn-out gown like that of a common countryman and a simple townsman’s cap, and then sent back to prison.

Cranmer was known for his gentle nature, but also for a natural timidity that he often had to fight against. His enemies knew this weakness well and decided to use it to disgrace him. After his trial at Oxford, he remained in close confinement, but was treated with more kindness than at any earlier time in his captivity.

He had longed to die with his friends, Latimer and Ridley, believing their example and company would strengthen and comfort him. But instead, he was forced to witness their martyrdom and left alone. Over time, the sense of abandonment—by both man and possibly even God—began to wear down his inner strength.

His captors noticed this change and planned to use it to make him deny his faith. As Archbishop of Canterbury and spiritual leader of the English Reformation, Cranmer’s recantation would be a powerful blow to Protestant believers. It would not only shame him but could also shake the faith of many others. The priests always intended to kill him, but they wanted him to go to death with the bitter knowledge that he had betrayed his faith for nothing.

They softened him with kind treatment, making him believe they truly wished to help him. In time, they convinced him that the queen and the Church would not only pardon him, but perhaps even restore him to favor—if he would sign a confession denying the truths he had once preached. Overcome by fear, the love of life, and horror at the tortures his friends had endured, Cranmer gave in during a moment of weakness. He signed a statement accepting the pope’s authority and the doctrine of Transubstantiation.

The Romanists, thrilled at their apparent victory, claimed he had surrendered completely. “Most likely,” writes Southey, “he signed an unclear or ambiguous recantation at first, and that the other five documents—where he supposedly endorsed doctrines he had often refuted and called himself a troublemaker and blasphemer—were forged under Bonner’s orders.” The whole situation was filled with doubt and confusion. What is certain is that Cranmer gave in, believing his life would be spared and that he could live quietly, without honor or wealth, wherever he wished.

A month passed after Cranmer’s recantation, during which he was left in uncertainty about his fate—even though it had already been decided. His enemies meant for this time to be filled with humiliation and inner torment, knowing that a man as pure and sensitive as Cranmer would feel deeply the weight of what he had done. And he did suffer—but that suffering, though bitter, brought spiritual healing.

During this time, Cranmer had the chance to reflect on his actions. He saw clearly how much damage his fear and denial could cause the Church. His conscience awakened, and by God's grace, he received the strength that his natural character lacked. His remorse was deep and painful, but it led to repentance rather than despair. Though he was not officially told of the Court’s plans, signs made it clear to him that he would be executed. He resolved to meet death with a courage born of faith.

On March 21, 1556, Cranmer was led out of prison to die. The usual sermon for such occasions was to have been preached at the stake, but because of wind and rain, it was delivered instead at St. Mary’s Church. The authorities fully expected Cranmer to repeat his recantation in front of the crowd, and the sermon was prepared accordingly.

Cranmer stood with solemn dignity. His face and bearing showed the deep sorrow and true repentance that filled his heart. Even his enemies felt pity. John Fox describes the scene: “Cranmer, during the sermon, revealed by his expressions more than words could say. At times he lifted his hands and eyes to heaven, then dropped them to the ground in shame. One could see in him the very image of heartfelt grief. More than twenty times tears streamed from his aged face. Those who were there testified they had never seen even a child weep so bitterly. The sight moved all who looked upon him, for in this old and honored man there was such a sorrowful countenance and such a flood of tears. Yet through it all, he remained calm and noble in his manner.”

Cranmer’s grief was not born of fear for his life, as his steady courage at the end clearly showed. It was the sorrow of deep repentance for denying his faith—a sorrow strong and pure, with no trace of weakness. He had conquered his fears. Had Queen Mary offered him life and freedom that very day in exchange for holding to his recantation, there is no doubt he would have refused, choosing instead to honor his Lord and seal his faith with his blood.

When it was his time to speak, Cranmer rose in the church and declared his submission to the queen and the laws of the land—but only in the sense of accepting whatever punishment they chose to inflict. His greater duty, he said, was to God, and that required him to speak the truth, not to deny the teachings God had entrusted to His Church.

“And now,” he said, “since I am come to the end of my life, upon which depends all that I have done and all that lies ahead—either to live with my Saviour Christ in joy, or to be forever tormented with the wicked in hell—and I see before my eyes either heaven”—and here he pointed upward—“or hell”—and pointed downward—“ready to receive me, I will declare to you my true faith, without disguise or deceit, for this is no time to dissemble.

"I believe in God the Father Almighty, Creator of heaven and earth; and in every part of the true Christian faith; in all that our Savior Jesus Christ taught, and in every word spoken by His apostles and prophets in the Old and New Testaments."

“And now I come to the thing that troubles my conscience more than anything I have ever said or done in my life,” Cranmer continued, “and that is the spreading of writings that went against the truth. I here renounce and reject them—those writings made by my own hand but contrary to what I truly believed in my heart. I wrote them in fear of death, hoping to save my life if it were possible. These include all the papers and statements I have signed since my degradation. In them I wrote many things that were false.

“And because my hand has sinned by writing against my heart, my hand shall be the first to suffer punishment. If I come to the fire, it shall be burned first. As for the Pope, I reject him utterly—as the enemy of Christ and Antichrist—with all his false teachings. And as for the Sacrament, I hold and believe just as I have taught in my book against the Bishop of Winchester.”

The Romanists were enraged by these words. Yet Cranmer’s heartfelt confession, clear and full of conviction, restored his honor in the eyes of many and confirmed his place as a faithful leader in the Reformed Church of England. The priests shouted him down and would not let him speak further. They hurried him away to the stake, where he met death with courage and faith, finishing his life as a true Christian and a steadfast witness for the truth.

Rome’s plan was defeated once again, and the cause of the gospel triumphed in the faith of its martyr. Just as Peter denied his Lord in weakness but later glorified Him in boldness, so Cranmer fell—but rose again, faithful to the end.

The martyrdom of Archbishop Cranmer dealt the greatest blow to the Roman Church of any execution during Mary’s reign. Thousands had witnessed his heartfelt repentance, and no statement from Bonner or the other clergy could convince them otherwise. Tens of thousands saw the calm courage with which he went to the stake. The entire nation grieved his death and was outraged by the cruelty shown to him. Even among those loyal to Rome, the event stirred horror.

Yet Cranmer’s blood only made his persecutors more ruthless. “Cranmer alone so far had been executed after a recantation,” wrote Froude. “To others, pardon had always been offered up to the last moment.” But now, even that small mercy was denied.

From the Cross and Crown, an upcoming audiobook.

title: How God Awakens His People—Even Through Pain
excerpt: It may be long since the Holy Spirit first woke us from the sleep of death. Into that deep sleep we know we shall never fall again. The One who woke us will keep us awake until Jesus comes. In that sense, we will sleep no more.
description: The following is Chapter 9 from Horatius Bonar's book: The Night of Weeping (an audiobook that is coming soon). It may be long since the Holy Spirit first woke us from the sleep of death. Into that deep sleep we know we shall never fall again. The One who woke us will keep us awake until Jesus comes. In that sense, we will sleep no more. Yet much of our drowsiness remains. We are not fully awake, and often the heaviness of our old sleep returns. Living on the world’s enchanted ground, our eyes grow heavy, our senses confused, our conscience dull, and our strength weak. We fall asleep even at our watchtower, forgetting that the night is almost over and the day is near. While asleep, or half-asleep, everything goes wrong. Our steps are slow and lifeless. Our faith grows weak, our love grows cold, and our zeal fades. The freshness of earlier years is gone. Our boldness leaves us. Our plans are careless, and our work sluggish. The cause of Christ is hindered by us instead of helped. We weigh it down and harm it. But God will not allow this. For the sake of his work and for the sake of his people, he will not let it continue. We must be awakened, whatever the cost. We cannot sleep like others. We must stay watchful and sober, for we are children of the light and of the day, not of the night or darkness. God will not permit us to waste life as though it were only for play. Duties done carelessly, prayers half-hearted, a life outwardly blameless but powerless and without influence, words wise perhaps but without depth or weight—these are things God will not tolerate in his saints. It is either the coldness of Sardis, to whom he says, “If you will not watch, I will come on you like a thief, and you will not know the hour I come upon you.” Or it is the lukewarmness of Laodicea, to whom he says, “Because you are lukewarm, neither cold nor hot, I will spit you out of my mouth.” In waking us, God begins gently. He touches us lightly, as the angel did Elijah under the tree, to stir us. He sends some small trial to shake us from our false rest. He lets us hear some distant sound—it may be the troubles of nations, or news of famine, war, or disease far away. If this fails, and we sleep on as before, he comes closer, and speaks through events nearer to home, perhaps among our own family. If this too fails, he comes still nearer, for time is short and his child is still asleep. He strikes at some tender spot until every nerve trembles and every heartbeat quickens. Our very soul is pierced through, as with many arrows. Then we start up like one waking from a long dream, and, looking around, wonder how we could have slept so long. But oh, how hard it is to truly wake us up! It takes blow after blow, over a long time, to do it. Even after we’ve been awakened, we constantly drift back to sleep. So we not only need to be awakened—we need to be kept awake. How much sorrow our spiritual sleepiness brings—how many broken, bleeding hearts! The comfort of “ease in Zion,” enjoyed perhaps for years, has come at a high cost. “Think of living,” said a thoughtful German. Another added, “Your life, even if you are the poorest soul on earth, is no idle dream, but a serious reality. It is your own. It’s all you have to face eternity with. So work like a star—unhurried, yet tireless.” Some Christians do work, but not like those who are truly awake. They carry out duties, but their steps are slow and heavy. Their efforts are forced and cold. They do good things, make good plans, say good words—but lack the vibrant energy of spiritual life. They have no burning zeal—no spring or strength in their labor. They don’t ignite others, and they themselves do not burn. The only thing star-like about them is their coldness. They are likely to face a sharp trial, because they need it. Others are only awake now and then. You can’t count on them, because their enthusiasm changes with their mood. Their naturally impulsive temper—which they may not even realize or try to discipline—makes them unstable. This kind of on-and-off wakefulness achieves little. They build up, then tear down. They ignite a flame, then put it out again. They too are not like stars. They need a deep, ongoing pressure to bring balance to their erratic spirit. Then there are those who always seem awake, but it’s just busy activity and restlessness. They can’t bear stillness. They must always be doing, planning, and moving. Their nervous, impatient nature makes silence and solitude feel like punishment. These people often don’t accomplish much themselves, but their constant activity stirs others and prevents stagnation. Still, their busyness keeps them from receiving the grace they need. Their endless involvement with outward religious things hinders their inward spiritual growth. They may seem like stars—restless and ever-moving—but they are losing the little light they had. They will likely need a hard, painful trial to strip away this false zeal and give them the steady spiritual wakefulness God calls them to. What they need is a deeper spiritual life, and that takes much refining. There are others who work steadily, and seem fervent too. But when you get to know them, you realize they’re not truly awake. They work far more than they pray, and so they soon become like lamps without oil. They may be further along than the previous group, but they still need awakening. They may resemble the star—calm and constant—but their light is dim. It shines faintly on the dark world. What they need is a deeper spiritual life and experience—and they too may face a hard trial to bring it about. The true awakened life is something else entirely. It is deep and intense. It has an atmosphere of calm strength and quiet dignity. It is passionate, but not frantic; active, but not rushed; wise, but not fearful or selfish; bold and firm, but not reckless. It doesn’t show off, and may even be silent—but those nearby can feel its influence. It is full of joy and peace, but without noise or display. It overflows with love and kindness, yet remains faithful and true. This is the wakeful life! But oh, how much we often have to suffer before we truly reach it. Our rebellious human nature resists full surrender to God. We struggle to become the living sacrifices He calls us to be—though it is our reasonable duty. In waking us from our spiritual sleep, God’s discipline doesn’t just make us more active or hardworking. It makes us far more prayerful. And perhaps this is where we feel the awakening most deeply. Nothing awakens prayer like trouble. It drives us straight to our knees, and closes the closet door behind us. In times of comfort, we have many distractions and sources of relief; in sorrow, we have only one—and that is God. Our grief is too deep to share with anyone else. No one else can carry it. Then we awaken to prayer. Before, it was something we did. Now, it becomes everything. Human help fails, and we are left leaning only on God. Our private prayer closets are truly the only places of light in a world that, to us, has grown doubly dark. Everything around us feels gloomy. The skies are clouded, and shadows cover all. But the closet is still bright—still peaceful. How eagerly, how gratefully we retreat there now. We could spend all our time in that quiet island of light that God has given us in the midst of life’s stormy sea. And when we must leave it for a time, how gladly we return. In that sacred solitude, with only God for company, we find hours of quiet peace. There, we can almost forget the storms still raging around us. Prayer becomes more real than ever. We now value it as we never did before. We cannot live without it. Out of both need and desire, we must pray—we must cry out from deep within. Our prayers are no longer formal or routine. Each one is now filled with real life, real energy, and deep earnestness. The heart prays now, and the lips can barely find words to express it. Often, it’s only the groanings that cannot be spoken which rise up to God. Before, we often had lips without the heart—now, more often, it is the heart without the lips. Now we begin to understand how “the Spirit helps our weaknesses.” We start to know what it means to “pray in the Holy Spirit.” There is a new closeness to God. Communion with Him becomes a clear, felt reality. It is deep, personal interaction with the living God. Fresh arguments rise in prayer, new desires are born, new needs come to light. Our emptiness and God’s rich fullness are shown to us more vividly than ever. The longings of our inmost soul are stirred, and our heart cries out for God—the living God. It was sorrow that awakened prayer in David. It was in the belly of the fish that Jonah learned to cry out to God. And it was in the thorny wilderness and the chains of Babylon that Manasseh learned to pray. Church of Christ—chosen people of the Lord—wake up! Children of the light, children of the day, rise! The long winter night is almost over. The morning star is about to rise. “The end of all things is at hand: therefore be sober, and watch unto prayer” (1 Peter 4:7). “Why are you sleeping? Rise and pray, so that you won’t fall into temptation!” (Luke 22:46).

title: Sending a Message with No Feet: The Cost of Trusting a Fool
description:
Proverbs 26:6-9 — Sending a message by the hand of a fool is like cutting off one’s own feet or drinking violence. Like lame legs that hang limp is a proverb in the mouth of fools. Like binding a stone in a sling is giving honor to a fool. Like a thorn that sticks into the hand of a drunkard is a proverb in the mouth of fools.

These pictures show just how dangerous and useless folly is, and they should stir us to pursue true wisdom. A fool is completely unfit for any important task. When we send a fool with a message, his errors—whether careless or deliberate—are so harmful it’s as if we’ve sent someone without feet. The result is injury and loss.

This was seen when the unbelieving spies spread doubt and rebellion among the people, causing great harm. It reminds us to place important responsibilities only in the hands of faithful, reliable people.

Fools either lack the ability to do their task or are driven by self-interest, even at the expense of those they serve. Solomon suffered harm by trusting an ambitious servant—who rebelled and took most of the kingdom from Solomon’s son. Ben-hadad was destroyed after sending a message through Hazael, who betrayed and murdered him to fulfill his own ambition.

See again how the fool exposes his shame. A lame man doesn’t show his weakness more than when he tries to display strength or agility. In the same way, a fool never looks more ridiculous than when he tries to appear wise. It’s off-putting.

A wise saying does not fit a fool—just like dancing doesn’t fit a cripple. A proverb—a serious, weighty saying—becomes a joke in his mouth. “Is Saul also among the prophets?” “Why do you look at the speck in your brother’s eye but ignore the beam in your own?” “Physician, heal yourself.” “You who teach others, do you not teach yourself?” These examples show how out of place wisdom sounds when spoken by the foolish.

Giving honor to a fool is just as dangerous. A stone tied into a sling becomes a deadly weapon. So does a fool given a place of honor—he becomes a danger to those around him.

One such fool, close to a powerful king, would have destroyed God’s chosen people—if God had not stepped in and stopped him. How dangerous it is to place unqualified people in power! It’s like handing a sword or a loaded gun to a madman.

The fool also harms himself—often without realizing it. Just as it is unfitting for a drunk man to grab a thorn bush, so it is for a fool to handle wise words. When a thorn pierces his hand, his numbness only makes the injury worse. In the same way, when a fool speaks a parable—a sharp, wise saying he barely understands—it pierces his own conscience. Yet he feels nothing.

What a sobering sight—the ungodly prophet speaking the very words of God with cold indifference, though those words are enough to make one tremble.

This is the fool: a danger to others, deeply accountable to God. And how much greater the danger and guilt when he takes on a sacred role! When the great message of God is delivered through ungodly messengers, the Church suffers serious harm.

The parable—the holy teaching of our Lord—when spoken by a fool, is twisted and contradicted by his life. “To the wicked God says: What right do you have to declare My statutes or take My covenant on your lips—since you hate instruction and cast My words behind you?”

O Almighty God, who alone does great wonders, send down upon all Bishops and ministers the life-giving power of Your grace. Raise up faithful stewards—workers unashamed—true ambassadors of Christ in Your Church. And may foolish, unfaithful ministers be corrected and restrained.

From Charles Bridges' expository commentary on Proverbs.

title: An Irish Flame for Christ: The Short, Burning Life of Thomas Walsh
description:
In contrast to Nelson’s robust humanity was the deeply spiritual and ascetic Thomas Walsh. Born into a devout Roman Catholic family and intended for the priesthood, Walsh exhibited an intense and contemplative nature. Yet his strict faith brought him no peace. At the age of eighteen, he heard a Methodist preacher speak on the text: “Come unto Me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.” This encounter marked the turning point of his remarkable spiritual journey.

Thomas Walsh’s life was marked by such extraordinary godliness and devotion that even those outside his communion could not deny the work of God in him. Dr. Southey famously remarked that Walsh’s life “might almost convince a Catholic that saints were to be found in other communions as well as in the Church of Rome.” This observation underscores the profound spiritual impact of Walsh, an Irishman whose commitment to God was matched by his remarkable intellect and scholarship. Walsh became a master of his native Irish, enabling him to preach to his people with clarity and passion, while also excelling in Latin, Greek, and Hebrew. His knowledge of Hebrew was so extensive that his memory operated as an entire concordance of the Bible, allowing him to connect and recall Scripture with extraordinary precision.

John Wesley spoke of him with high regard: “I do not remember ever to have known a man who, in so few years as he remained upon earth, was the instrument of converting so many sinners.” Walsh’s ministry had a tremendous impact, particularly among Roman Catholics, whose priests claimed that Walsh had died years earlier and that the man preaching in mountains, fields, and prisons was a devil in disguise. This rumor only attests to the extraordinary influence he wielded.

Walsh’s fervent faith burned intensely, but his body could not endure the strain. He labored between Ireland and London, leaving a trail of profound conversions. Yet, he died young, at only twenty-seven years old. In his final days, he suffered deeply with doubts about his salvation. On his deathbed, after intense mental agony, he suddenly cried out, “He is come! He is come! My Beloved is mine, and I am His forever!” With this triumphant exclamation, he passed away. Walsh remains one of the most revered figures among the early Methodist lay preachers.

title: Punishing Accidents: The Parenting Mistake That Confuses Children
excerpt: Never punish a child for what he did not mean to do.
description:
Never punish a child for what he did not mean to do. Secondly, never punish a child for what he did not mean to do. Children are often punished unfairly. Sometimes true faults are ignored, while other times a child is punished for something that was purely accidental. This not only confuses the child—blurring the line between guilt and misfortune—but it is also a deep injustice. Parents hold great power. But when they misuse that power—especially in anger—they become tyrants in the eyes of their children, and the children have no way to defend themselves. There is no oppression more cruel than the unfair punishment of a helpless child. Often, a mother may not intend to be unjust. But in the moment, she fails to make the needed distinction between a fault and an accident. A child may tear his clothes or break a window while playing with a ball—a ball the mother had allowed him to use. Irritated by the trouble this causes, she reacts in anger and punishes the child. Yes, carelessness can be blameworthy. But if the child is to be punished, it must be clear that it is the carelessness—not the accident—that is being corrected. To punish a child for something he did not mean to do is a deep wrong, and far more common than most parents realize. In fact, the most frequent cause of unjust punishment is when a parent confuses the consequences of an action with the intent behind it. We must never forget: it is the heart and motive that determine guilt—not the outcome of the act. Parents are often quick to punish carelessness when it leads to damage or injury, yet they tend to overlook that same carelessness when nothing goes wrong. For instance, a girl may be trusted to watch an infant but leaves for a moment to play. If the baby remains unharmed, the mother might only give a light scolding. But if the infant falls and gets hurt, the girl is punished—though the fault in both cases is the same: neglect of duty. We are all too likely to judge guilt by the outcome. A child, for example, has been allowed to climb on chairs and reach for things on the table. One day he does so and accidentally knocks over something valuable. The mother, upset by the loss, punishes him harshly. But where was the wrongdoing? He had not been taught that climbing on the table was forbidden, and in that case, he was not disobeying. If the child had only knocked off a book, the mother likely would have said nothing. The object that happened to fall—whether valuable or not—does not change the moral nature of the act. Even if the item were an expensive watch, and even if it were ruined, if the fall was truly accidental, the child does not deserve punishment. Some might say this is obvious and needs no further proof. But is it not true that such injustices happen often? And is not nearly every mother aware that she sometimes fails to make this distinction? A parent must have steady control over her emotions—calmness that is not easily shaken—or she will occasionally be unjust toward her children simply because she is upset by the consequences of a child's innocent mistake. What should be done in such situations? The answer is clear: children must be taught not to do things that could cause damage. Then, if they disobey that rule—whether or not damage occurs—they can be rightly corrected for the disobedience itself. Returning to the earlier example, if the mother has warned her child not to climb on the table, and the child does it anyway, he has clearly disobeyed, even if nothing had been broken. And if something is broken, the punishment must still be for the disobedience—not for the accident. A wise and loving mother would calmly say:
“My son, I have told you that you must not climb on the table. You have disobeyed me.”
And when the son replies,
“But, mother, I didn’t mean to do any harm,”
She answers,
“I believe you. I’m not punishing you for breaking anything—I know that was an accident. But you disobeyed me, and that is wrong. I am very sorry to punish you, but it is my duty.”
She may then choose a fitting consequence—perhaps a loss of a privilege, or some other form of correction—but it will be clear to the child that he is being disciplined for disobedience, not for an accident. He may feel the pain of correction, but he will also feel the fairness of it. But what should a mother do if the child was never taught that climbing on the table or throwing a ball indoors was wrong? In that case, clearly, she has no right to rebuke the child. The fault lies with her for failing to teach him beforehand. Now, all she can rightly do is to use the moment as a teaching opportunity—to explain the danger of such behavior and clearly forbid it for the future. If the child is very young, the mother will need to repeat this instruction from time to time. She should refer back to the incident and to the rule she established because of it, so the lesson stays fresh in the child's memory. Without reminders, a small child might forget and return to the same behavior, not out of rebellion but out of ignorance. We must also be patient with a child's lack of understanding. Imagine you have a daughter who is eighteen months old. She has often played by tearing up old newspapers you've given her. One day, while you're not paying attention, she quietly tears up an expensive book. Your first reaction might be to scold or punish her. But has she really done something wrong? No. She doesn't yet know the difference between a useless paper and a valuable book. To her, it's the same thing she’s always been allowed to do. In such a case, she should not be punished but gently taught. She can learn by the serious tone of your voice and the sad look on your face that something is wrong. She will begin to understand that books must be handled carefully. Over time, she will learn the difference between what she is allowed to play with and what she must not touch. A similar example occurred when a boy, about two years old, often amused himself by scribbling with a pencil on loose paper. One day, his father found him marking all over the pages of a new book. The child had no idea he was doing anything wrong and continued cheerfully when his father entered the room. Many parents would have taken the book away angrily and slapped the child out of frustration. But this father calmly walked over and said gently:
“Oh, my son, you’re spoiling the book.”
The boy looked up in surprise.
“That’s a book, my son. You mustn’t write in it. Look here—you’re making marks that will ruin it. Here’s some paper for you. You may write on this, but never in a book.”
The father then quietly set the book aside without showing anger. This calm and thoughtful response was not only just but deeply instructive. And yet, how few children are spared from undeserved punishment in such moments. These examples show how important it is to make room for both ignorance and accidents. Children often suffer correction when they are not truly at fault. But if a child is punished regardless of whether he is innocent or guilty, he will soon lose the ability to distinguish right from wrong. Therefore, one of the key principles of family discipline is this: never punish a child unless he has knowingly and intentionally done wrong.
